text: Chlorophylls are essential dietary antioxidants found in green vegetables. In this study, we investigated the specific mechanisms by which the $a$ and $b$ forms of chlorophyll and pheophytin (its magnesium-dechelated derivative) reduce oxidative stress. Using the comet assay, we demonstrated that these compounds protect human lymphocytes from DNA damage induced by hydrogen peroxide ($H_2O_2$). We also evaluated their chemical antioxidant capacity through DPPH radical scavenging and their ability to chelate ferrous iron [Fe(II)], a key catalyst in the formation of reactive oxygen species. Furthermore, we measured their effectiveness in preventing lipid peroxidation by monitoring thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) during copper-mediated oxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Our results revealed that all tested chlorophylls and pheophytins exhibit significant, dose-dependent antioxidant activity, with the pheophytins emerging as the most potent variants. These findings suggest that chlorophyll-related compounds guard against oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation through a dual mechanism: the direct scavenging of free radicals and the chelation of pro-oxidant metal ions.
